title: Improving Federal Financial Management Act
stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
plain_summary: This bill makes changes to how the government handles money and keeps track of spending. It requires the Office of Management and Budget to create a four-year plan for improving how all government agencies manage their finances and report this plan to Congress. The bill also gives Chief Financial Officers at government agencies more jobs to do, like overseeing budgets, managing risks, and making sure their agencies follow the government-wide financial plan. The goal is to make the federal government better at managing taxpayer money and keeping accurate records of how it is spent.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Improving Federal Financial Management Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Chief Financial Officers Governmentwide Financial Management Plan
sections.text:
This section changes the rules for Chief Financial Officers in government agencies and how the government makes financial management plans.

First, it updates the duties of Chief Financial Officers. Each agency Chief Financial Officer must oversee and lead in several areas including making budgets, planning performance, managing risks, controlling internal processes, running financial systems, doing accounting, and other areas that the Office of Management and Budget decides. The Chief Financial Officer must also prepare annual financial statements that follow standard accounting rules.

Each Chief Financial Officer must create a plan for their agency within ninety days after the Office of Management and Budget releases the government-wide plan. This agency plan must show how the agency will carry out the four year government financial management plan and achieve good financial management. The plan must include ways to measure financial performance and must be shared with the agency head, the Office of Management and Budget, the Comptroller General, and Congress committees. The plan must also be made available to the public.

Chief Financial Officers must report each year to their agency head, the Office of Management and Budget, the Comptroller General, and Congress committees about their agency's financial management. These reports must be made public and must include progress on the agency plan, progress on the government-wide plan, and how well the agency is doing on financial management measures.

Chief Financial Officers must work with other senior agency staff including the Chief Data Officer, Chief Information Officer, Chief Performance Officer, Chief Acquisition Officer, Chief Risk Officer, and Chief Evaluation Officer on planning, measuring performance, reporting, and managing risks.

The section also says that if a Chief Financial Officer position becomes empty, the Deputy Chief Financial Officer will serve as the acting Chief Financial Officer.

Second, it changes how the government makes financial management plans. Instead of making five year plans, the Office of Management and Budget must now make four year plans. These plans must describe actions to improve federal financial management in a way that is strategic, comprehensive, and cost effective. The plans must be developed by working with various councils and experts including the Chief Financial Officers Council and the Government Accountability Office.

The four year plan must include strategies for improving financial systems, linking performance and cost information, eliminating duplicate systems, and encouraging agencies to share systems when practical. It must provide strategies for reporting performance and cost information and for strengthening the federal financial management workforce. The plan must include comprehensive ways to measure financial management performance.

The Office of Management and Budget must also prepare annual financial management status reports. These reports must describe the status of financial management in the executive branch, including progress on the four year plan, remaining challenges, and cost estimate updates. The reports must summarize how agencies are performing against the established measures and provide summaries of financial statements and audits.

The timing for these plans and reports is also changed. The first four year plan must be submitted within six months after this law is passed. New four year plans must be submitted at least when a new President's term begins. Annual status reports must be submitted each year with the government budget.

Third, the section requires agency heads to identify the key financial management information needed for good financial management and decision making. Agency heads must assess each year how effective their internal controls are over financial reporting and key financial management information.

Finally, the section updates audit requirements. When auditors review agency financial statements, they must evaluate the design of internal controls over financial reporting, determine if those controls have been put in place, test the controls to see if they work effectively, and report on any controls that are not working properly.
